votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the logic of the Coq specification, including whitespace handling, character replacement, string splitting, and counting sentences that start with 'I ' after trimming.

The Python code correctly implements the logic of the Coq specification: it replaces '?' and '!' with '.', splits the string by '.', strips whitespace from each part, and counts how many parts start with 'I '. The helper functions for whitespace, stripping, and counting align with the Coq definitions.

The Python code accurately implements the Coq specification. The string transformations (replacing '?'/'!' with '.', splitting by '.', and stripping whitespace) and the counting logic ('boredom' predicate) correspond exactly to the Coq definitions. The ambiguity in Coq's splitting of empty strings does not affect the final count, ensuring concrete equivalence.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification for concrete inputs. It replaces '?' and '!' with '.', splits on '.', and counts parts that start with 'I ' after stripping whitespace. The key functions (replace_rel, strip_rel, boredom_on_part, count_sat, is_bored_spec) all produce the same concrete results as their Coq counterparts. The split_on_dot check is always true when parts come from S1.split('.') since Python's split naturally produces parts without dots, and joining them with '.' reconstructs S1.

Python mirrors the Coq relations: same whitespace set and trimming, same mapping of ?/! to ., same dot intercalation/splitting, boredom_on_part checks trimmed parts start with "I ", and count equals the number of such parts. The existential choices in Coq are satisfied by the deterministic choices in Python, yielding the same results on all inputs.

The Python code implements the same concrete behaviors: ASCII whitespace trimming, per-char replacement of ?/! with ., splitting on dots into dot-free parts, checking for trimmed parts starting with "I ", and counting such parts. The intercalation-based split is uniquely determined by dots, so the existential in Coq matches Python’s canonical split. Thus results coincide on all inputs.

The Python code mirrors the Coq spec’s concrete behavior: whitespace checks match ASCII codes; strip_rel trims leading/trailing whitespace to the unique middle t; replace_rel replaces '?' and '!' with '.' deterministically; splitting on '.' with parts having no '.' matches the unique decomposition; boredom_on_part checks trimmed parts start with 'I '; and count_sat sums these. is_bored_spec uses the canonical S1 and parts satisfying the existential. Thus results align on all concrete inputs.
